common_name: Nun's Orchid (Swamp Orchid, Veiled Nun Orchid, Limestone Orchid)

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs or container-grown divisions in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil or orchid mix; Phaius tankervilleae is one of the largest and most cold-tolerant terrestrial orchids for zone 9-10 garden use — established clumps have survived brief temperatures to 28°F with heavy mulch in zone 9a. The tall flower spikes (2–4 feet) bearing 10–20 large flowers make this one of the most spectacular orchids for outdoor garden planting. Divide clumps every 3–4 years. Needs consistent moisture during active growth (spring–fall); reduce watering in winter. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Nun's Orchid grows best in the consistently moist, organically rich conditions of a shaded or partially shaded garden bed under large trees, where its tall flower spikes create an outstanding spring display. Phaius tankervilleae is a large, clump-forming terrestrial orchid native from India through Southeast Asia, China, Papua New Guinea to Australia and many Pacific Islands in the Orchidaceae family — one of the most spectacular terrestrial orchids available for outdoor garden planting in zone 9-11 subtropical gardens. The plant produces large, egg-shaped pseudobulbs and tall, broad, pleated leaves (18–30 inches) that create a lush, tropical-looking clump. In late winter to spring, the pseudobulbs send up one to several tall, erect flower spikes 2–4 feet tall, each bearing 10–20 large (3-inch) flowers in sequence from the bottom up. Each flower has an unusual, distinctive color combination: the outer surface of the sepals and petals is white to pale cream, while the inner surface is a rich deep brown-maroon to chocolate-brown with a white-edged, rose-purple to magenta lip — the combination of white outside and dark inside that inspired the common name 'Nun's Orchid' for the resemblance to a nun's habit. The tall spikes with their two-toned flowers are impressive in the shade garden. Naturalized in parts of Florida and considered invasive in Hawaii — check local regulations. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Phaius tankervilleae for Florida outdoor gardens
LSU AgCenter — Cold-Hardy Terrestrial Orchids for Gulf Coast Louisiana Zone 9
American Orchid Society — Phaius tankervilleae outdoor cultivation guide
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Terrestrial orchids for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens

common_name: Darwin's Orchid (Star of Bethlehem Orchid, Comet Orchid, Christmas Orchid)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork bark or in a slatted basket with coarse bark medium in bright indirect to filtered morning sun; Angraecum sesquipedale blooms in winter (November–January), triggered by seasonal temperature drops to 55–60°F at night — this natural temperature fluctuation is essential for inducing bloom. Protect from temperatures below 50°F. In zone 9, grow on a shaded porch in a basket from spring through early fall, then move inside to a bright, cool windowsill for the winter bloom period. Water regularly in growth; reduce slightly in winter. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to gentle filtered light in a well-draining bark medium or mounted on cork — Darwin's Orchid grows best in bright, diffuse light similar to dappled forest canopy and will not bloom reliably in dim interior conditions. Angraecum sesquipedale is a large, monopodial epiphytic orchid endemic to Madagascar in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most historically significant plants in all of evolutionary biology — the subject of Charles Darwin's famous 1862 prediction. Examining dried herbarium specimens of A. sesquipedale with their extraordinary flower structure — large (5-inch), waxy, pure white, six-pointed, star-shaped flowers with a single, enormously elongated, thin, whip-like nectar spur hanging below the flower to a length of 11–13 inches — Darwin proposed in his book 'Fertilisation of Orchids' that somewhere in Madagascar, an undiscovered moth must exist with a proboscis long enough to reach the nectar at the base of the spur. Critics ridiculed the prediction. In 1903, 21 years after Darwin's death, a subspecies of the hawk moth Xanthopan morganii was discovered in Madagascar with a proboscis of exactly the predicted length — the confirmation of a prediction from evolutionary logic. The flowers are intensely fragrant at night, attracting their hawk moth pollinators. A large, impressive orchid with stunning winter flowers and extraordinary scientific heritage. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Angraecum sesquipedale: Darwin's Orchid cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Angraecum sesquipedale: Darwin's prediction
Missouri Botanical Garden — Angraecum sesquipedale species profile
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Angraecum (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Coconut Orchid (Thin-Leaved Maxillaria, Red Coconut Orchid)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork bark or in a basket with coarse bark medium in bright indirect to filtered direct morning light; Maxillaria tenuifolia is tolerant of a wider range of conditions than many epiphytic orchids and adapts well to intermediate indoor or shaded porch culture. Water regularly during active growth; reduce watering in winter but do not allow to desiccate completely. The coconut fragrance of the flowers is strongest on warm, sunny days and carries several feet from the plant. Blooms spring–summer. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light in coarse bark medium or mounted on cork — Coconut Orchid needs good light to bloom reliably but benefits from protection from the most intense direct summer sun that can scorch the thin leaves. Maxillaria tenuifolia is a small, creeping, sympodial epiphytic orchid native to Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most beloved small orchids in the collector world for a single, unforgettable characteristic: the flowers smell exactly, unmistakably, and powerfully of fresh coconut — a sweet, creamy, tropical fragrance that seems impossible from a small orchid flower but is real and carries several feet from the plant on warm days. The plant itself is modest in stature: a creeping mass of small, oval-to-egg-shaped pseudobulbs each producing a single, narrow (the 'tenuifolia' — thin-leaved), grass-like leaf 8–14 inches long. In spring and early summer, individual flowers emerge one per pseudobulb on very short stems from among the leaves — each flower 1–1.5 inches across, dark red to maroon-red with cream to yellow spotting on the lip, the overall coloration somewhat dark and modest in contrast to the extraordinary fragrance. A container or mounted specimen in full coconut bloom is one of the most pleasant aromatic experiences available from a tropical orchid. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Maxillaria tenuifolia: Coconut Orchid cultivation
Royal Horticultural Society — Maxillaria tenuifolia cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Small Epiphytic Orchids for indoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Maxillaria (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Two-Lobed Aerangis (Bilobed Aerangis, West African Star Orchid)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork bark or wood with exposed aerial roots in bright indirect to filtered direct light; Aerangis biloba is a miniature, monopodial epiphyte that thrives when the roots are fully exposed to air, drying between waterings. Mist or dunk the roots daily in summer; water weekly in winter. Provide good air circulation — still, humid air promotes fungal issues. The pendant racemes of fragrant, white, nocturnally-fragrant flowers bloom in fall and winter. Protect from temperatures below 55°F. Excellent as a terrarium or windowsill orchid. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow mounted on cork or wood with freely exposed aerial roots in bright indirect to filtered direct light — Aerangis biloba is a miniature monopodial epiphyte that performs best when mounted without any medium, allowing its silver roots to dry between mistings in the manner of its natural tree-branch habitat. Aerangis biloba is a small, monopodial epiphytic orchid from West Africa (Cameroon, Nigeria, Ivory Coast, Ghana, Sierra Leone) in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most rewarding of the approximately 50 Aerangis species for beginning and experienced orchid collectors. The plant is compact: a single upright stem with overlapping ranks of 4–8 oblong, dark-green, strap-shaped leaves 4–6 inches long with two rounded lobes at the tip (the 'biloba' — two-lobed). The aerial roots are silvery-white, thick, flat, and numerous. In fall and winter, the plant produces pendant racemes 8–16 inches long carrying 8–18 flowers in two ranks along the stem — each flower a small (1-inch), pure white, star-shaped bloom of the Angraecoid type (6 pointed petals in a flat arrangement with a long spur). The flowers open pure white and are intensely, sweetly fragrant at night. The pendant display of pristine white flowers on an arching stem, combined with the night fragrance, makes Aerangis biloba one of the most delightful of the African miniature orchids for indoor or protected porch culture. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Aerangis biloba cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Aerangis biloba species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Aerangis cultivation for miniature orchid collections
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Aerangis (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: West Indian Vanilla (Pompona Vanilla, Vanillón, Big-Leaved Vanilla)

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings (3–5 nodes, leafless, air-dried 1–2 days) in late spring in partial shade on a rough-textured wooden trellis, tree, or vertical support with moist, well-drained orchid bark or organic medium; Vanilla pompona is a hemi-epiphytic vine that climbs by aerial roots and produces the best results when growing up a natural surface. Hand-pollinate the short-lived flowers with a toothpick (pollen must be transferred to the stigma within the flower's single-day lifespan). The pods are shorter and thicker than V. planifolia but contain good vanilla flavor compounds. Frost-sensitive, zone 10-12 outdoors.

directions: Plant in partial shade to bright indirect light in moist, well-drained, organic-rich medium — West Indian Vanilla climbs most vigorously on rough-textured supports in conditions that approximate its native tropical forest habitat: bright, filtered light, consistent warmth, and humid air with good drainage at the roots. Vanilla pompona is a hemi-epiphytic climbing orchid vine native to Mexico, Central America, the Caribbean islands, and northern South America in the Orchidaceae family, closely related to the commercially dominant Vanilla planifolia but producing shorter, thicker, more barrel-shaped vanilla pods with a distinctly different, somewhat sweeter aromatic profile. As with all Vanilla species, the vine climbs by clasping aerial roots to tree bark or other rough surfaces, can reach impressive lengths (30+ feet), and produces axillary clusters of the characteristic large (2-inch), greenish-yellow to pale yellow vanilla orchid flowers — each lasting only a single day, requiring prompt hand pollination in cultivation to set the pods. The pods (the 'vanilla beans' of commerce) take 9–12 months to mature after pollination, then must be cured through a weeks-long process of sweating, drying, and conditioning to develop the characteristic vanilla aroma. Vanilla pompona has been used as an alternative and supplement to V. planifolia in commercial vanilla production, particularly in the Caribbean, and is more resistant to the Fusarium root rot disease that threatens V. planifolia plantations. An interesting food plant for zone 10-12 tropical gardens with appropriate climbing structures.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Vanilla species for Florida food gardens
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Vanilla pompona cultivation notes
American Orchid Society — Vanilla cultivation: species and hybrids
International Society for Horticultural Science — Vanilla pompona: cultivation and yield
